channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: First Round of U.S.-Iran Talks Ends With High Hopes and Big Challenges
item.description: Mediators reported progress toward reaching a final deal within 60 days. They also said that negotiators had dwelled on issues that were supposed to be settled.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Vice President JD Vance talking with officials from Pakistan and Qatar at the meeting in Switzerland on Sunday.

item.title: Israel Holds to Lebanon Truce, With Troops Kept on Defense
item.description: Israel and Hezbollah, the Iran-backed Lebanese militant group, appeared to maintain a tense cease-fire for a second day. Israel’s military has new orders that restrict troops to defensive actions.
item.dc:creator: David M. Halbfinger and Natan Odenheimer
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Recovering belongings from the rubble of a destroyed home near Nabatieh, Lebanon, on Monday.

item.title: U.S. Oil Is Skipping the Chance to Grab Market Share From the Gulf
item.description: U.S. oil production is expected to grow only modestly next year as companies hesitate to spend more in an uncertain market.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: Smaller U.S. oil producers are starting to drill more wells, but big companies like Exxon Mobil and Chevron have said they are not changing their plans as a result of the war with Iran.

item.title: Keir Starmer Resigns as UK Prime Minister
item.description: His departure in the coming weeks clears a path for Andy Burnham, a popular Labour Party mayor, to become the country’s seventh prime minister in a decade.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.media:description: Prime Minister Keir Starmer outside 10 Downing Street in London where he announced his resignation on Monday.

item.title: Who Is Andy Burnham, the Man Who Could Be Britain’s Next Prime Minister?
item.description: Charismatic, northern and exuding a relaxed optimism, Mr. Burnham is a contrast to Keir Starmer. His allies hope he could mend Labour’s relationship with voters.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Castle
item.media:description: Andy Burnham, Labour’s most popular politician, at his campaign launch for the Makerfield by-election in Ashton-in-Makerfield, England, in May.

item.title: Supreme Court Restores Conviction in 1979 Murder of Etan Patz
item.description: Pedro Hernandez was convicted in 2017 of kidnapping and murdering the 6-year-old boy, but an appeals court in July had said Mr. Hernandez was entitled to a new trial.
item.dc:creator: Hurubie Meko and Ann E. Marimow
item.media:description: Etan Patz was abducted in Manhattan in 1979. His case helped usher in an era of heightened caution among American parents.

item.title: Why Older People Are Divorcing More Than They Used To
item.description: Rates of gray divorce have risen sharply over the past few decades — and experts have a few theories as to why.
item.dc:creator: Catherine Pearson

item.title: Alan Greenspan, Fed Chairman Through Prosperity and Crisis, Dies at 100
item.description: The pre-eminent economic policymaker of his time and a skilled political operator, he favored market-friendly stances that would later come to be associated with destructive financial forces.
item.dc:creator: Richard W. Stevenson
item.media:description: Alan Greenspan in 1987. After overseeing a period of immense wealth creation, he was often portrayed as among those responsible for the 2008 crisis and the economic and political shocks that followed it.

item.title: In Alan Greenspan, Kevin Warsh Saw a Fed Chairman Role Model
item.description: Kevin M. Warsh has long singled out Alan Greenspan, who died on Monday, as the central banker he wants to exemplify.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith and Ben Casselman
item.media:description: Alan Greenspan at his 1987 confirmation hearing as the nominee for Federal Reserve chairman. He was known for being hard to parse as a leader of the central bank.

item.title: Congressional Democrats Warn Pulte Against Mass Firings
item.description: Current and former officials say the acting director of national intelligence is planning to announce major cuts to his office as early as Monday.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes
item.category: Russian Interference in 2016 US Elections and Ties to Trump Associates
Office of the Director of National Intelligence
item.media:description: The ascension of Bill Pulte to the position of acting director of national intelligence has caused a storm on Capitol Hill.

item.title: Judge Finds Justice Dept. Overreach in Subpoenas to Minnesota Officials
item.description: The judge quashed the subpoenas, writing that they were designed to “harass and retaliate against” Democratic officials who bucked the Trump administration on immigration enforcement.
item.dc:creator: Mitch Smith
item.media:credit: Carlos Gonzalez/Minnesota Star Tribune, via Associated Press
item.media:description: The U.S. District Court for the District of Minnesota in Minneapolis.

item.title: Justice Department Makes It Easier to Bypass Pollution Controls on Pickups
item.description: It has stopped criminal prosecutions of people who install “defeat devices,” which make diesel trucks faster and more efficient but also dirtier.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:credit: Acacia Johnson for The New York Times

item.title: Interior Department Moves to Relax Rules for Drilling on Public Lands
item.description: A pair of proposals from the Trump administration would lower costs and weaken requirements for fossil fuel companies that drill on federal property.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Paul Ratje for The New York Times
item.media:description: One proposal would lower the cost of the bonds that fossil fuel companies must pay the Interior Department before they start drilling or mining on public lands.

item.title: Clive Davis, Music Industry Titan Who Signed Whitney Houston, Dies at 94
item.description: He rose from a midlevel position at Columbia Records to become one of music’s most powerful executives, shepherding stars like Barry Manilow and Whitney Houston.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:credit: Bryan Derballa for The New York Times
item.media:description: One of the few executives in music to become a household name, Clive Davis maintained a visible role as a starmaker for more than five decades.

item.title: 11 Essential Songs Shepherded by Clive Davis
item.description: The label boss, who died on Monday, had a passion for hits — especially ones he masterminded — during his formidable tenures at Columbia, Arista and J Records.
item.dc:creator: Rob Tannenbaum
item.category: If You Don't Know Me by Now (Song)
item.media:description: After Clive Davis, right, assured Carlos Santana that “Smooth” would be the most important song on the album “Supernatural,” it spent three months at No. 1.

item.title: He Provided Security to 3 Governors. Then He Snapped.
item.description: Lt. Ricardo Santos of the New Jersey State Police had faced questions about his judgment and conduct before he became the primary suspect in a double murder-suicide.
item.dc:creator: Tracey Tully
item.media:credit: Kristin Callahan/Ace Pictures/ZUMA Press, via Alamy
item.media:description: Lt. Ricardo Santos of the New Jersey State Police worked in an elite unit protecting dignitaries.

item.title: YouTube Stars Take Center Stage at Creative Artists Agency
item.description: For years, creators were on the fringes at Creative Artists Agency, a Hollywood talent behemoth. Now the agency is putting them center stage.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes
item.media:credit: Michelle Groskopf for The New York Times
item.media:description: Trisha Paytas, a digital star, is part of CAA’s growing roster of creators.

item.title: ‘The View’ Asks Its Audience for Help in Battle With F.C.C.
item.description: The daytime TV talk show is at the center of a dispute with the Federal Communications Commission over political speech.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Mullin
item.media:description: From left, Whoopi Goldberg, Sara Haines, Joy Behar, Ana Navarro, Sunny Hostin and Alyssa Farah Griffin on the set of “The View.”

item.title: How Will Colombia’s New President Fuel Trump’s Drug War?
item.description: Abelardo De La Espriella, who has vowed to destroy traffickers with military power, appears set to be the country’s next president.
item.dc:creator: Luis Ferré-Sadurní
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Abelardo de la Espriella on Sunday night in Barranquilla, Colombia, after preliminary results showed him to be the winner in the presidential runoff.

item.title: Abelardo De La Espriella, Right-Wing Outsider, Could Be Colombia’s Next President
item.description: President Trump endorsed Abelardo De La Espriella, the conservative presidential candidate and apparent winner of Sunday’s election who has strong U.S. ties.
item.dc:creator: Annie Correal, Patricia Mazzei and Jorge Valencia
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: A campaign rally last month in Medellín, Colombia, for Abelardo De La Espriella, the right-wing presidential candidate.

item.title: Thousands Are Fleeing as Putin Bombs the Donbas Cities He Most Covets
item.description: While Kyiv’s fortunes have brightened in other ways in the war, Moscow’s forces are raining bombs and drones on “fortress belt” cities like Sloviansk and Kramatorsk.
item.dc:creator: Cassandra Vinograd and Oleksandr Chubko
item.media:credit: Finbarr O’Reilly for The New York Times
item.media:description: Evacuees from Sloviansk and Kramatorsk, in the Donetsk region of Ukraine, waited to be processed at a transit hub in Lozova, Ukraine, last month.

item.title: Ukraine Escalates Crimea Attacks, Creating a New Pressure Point for Russia
item.description: To pressure Moscow, Ukraine is stepping up an air campaign to isolate the peninsula, which Russia annexed in 2014.
item.dc:creator: Paul Sonne and Oleg Matsnev
item.media:description: A line for gas in Simferopol, Crimea, this month.

item.title: For Schlossberg, Quirky Charm and a Claim to Camelot May Not Be Enough
item.description: Jack Schlossberg, the grandson of John F. Kennedy, has found it difficult to overcome some of his perceived shortcomings in a high-profile House primary race.
item.dc:creator: Emma Goldberg and Claire Fahy
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jack Schlossberg, at a recent campaign event in Manhattan, has fallen to third place in recent polls of the Democratic primary in the 12th Congressional District.

item.title: What I Learned About Masculinity at Thai Kickboxing School
item.description: A week inside a grueling Muay Thai training program in Phuket.
item.dc:creator: Dan Brooks and Thomas Prior

item.title: Ten Years After Brexit, the Dismal Verdict Is In
item.description: Brexit was supposed to let Britain return to a time when it still counted as a global power. A decade later, the costs are blindingly apparent.
item.dc:creator: Philip Stephens

item.title: The Moment Alan Greenspan Should Be Remembered For
item.description: A crucial episode in the famous Fed chair’s legacy.
item.dc:creator: Roger Lowenstein

item.title: Behind the Wheel
item.description: We look into the dangers posed by large S.U.V.s and pickup trucks.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton
item.category: Sports Utility Vehicles and Light Trucks

item.title: A Loophole Brings Cystic Fibrosis Patients a ‘Miracle Drug’ in Generic Form
item.description: A generic version of a breakthrough cystic fibrosis drug, manufactured in Bangladesh for a fraction of the American price, may give some families around the world an unlikely lifeline.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Nolen
item.media:credit: Fabeha Monir for The New York Times
item.media:description: Josua Lottering traveled with his mother from South Africa to Bangladesh to buy a life-changing drug for cystic fibrosis.
